method: author self-audit (not human validation, not independent verification): the implementing author read each sampled context line and manually judged whether the regex-parsed (entity, predicate, value) triple matches the text of that line exactly.
note: Every sampled (entity, predicate, value) triple exactly matched its source context line -- the regex correctly extracted what the line literally says, including several factually implausible MQuAKE-style counterfactual edits (e.g. "Ernst Heinkel is a citizen of Tang Empire", "Zinedine Zidane plays the position of linebacker") which are NOT parser errors: the parser is not supposed to fact-check, only to extract the stated triple, and it did so correctly in every sampled case. This bounds only PARSER EXTRACTION precision, not the plausibility or real-world truth of MemoryAgentBench content, and not the correctness of any downstream ground-truth label built from it (Section 3.5, Limitations). At n=50 with 0 observed errors, the 95% Clopper-Pearson upper bound on the true error rate is 7.1%, i.e. this self-audit supports precision >= ~93% with 95% confidence, not a proof of exactly 100% over the full 51,314-triple population.
